petitioner_background: The petitioner is a Chinese female volleyball player seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in athletics under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act. She was a member of the Chinese National Women's Volleyball Team that competed at the 1992 Barcelona Olympic Games (placing 7th) and won various medals at Chinese national championships and international competitions between 1986 and 1994. She later attended Fresno Pacific University in the United States on a student visa, where she played NAIA volleyball and received various collegiate honors, and is pursuing a degree in international business/trade/commerce.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted evidence of medals won at several Chinese championships between 1986 and 1994, including the Chinese National Championships in 1986, 1988, 1989, 1990, 1991, 1992 and 1994, and the Seventh National Games in 1993. She also submitted evidence of medals at international competitions including the 1991 World Cup in Japan, the International Challenge competition in 1992, the Asia Championships in 1993, and the World Championships in Hong Kong in 1993. In the U.S., as a Fresno Pacific University student in the NAIA, she submitted evidence of: Most Valuable Player NAIA 2001 and 2002; 2003 NAIA Volleyball Championship; 2002 Volleyball Champion Golden State Athletic Conference; Member NAIA All-Tournament Team 1999, 2001 and 2002; Member AVCA All-America Team 1999, 2001 and 2002; Final Four NAIA National Championship 2001 and 2002; Member 1999 All Regional Team Region II; and Runner Up National Championship 1999. She also submitted certificates from the American Volleyball Coaches Association and team plaques from Fresno Pacific University. Counsel also initially asserted the petitioner won a silver medal at the 1992 Barcelona Olympic Games, but an unsigned list from the China Volleyball Association showed she placed 7th; she submitted photocopies of Olympic credentials and an Olympic button but no photo of a silver medal or media coverage.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the Chinese championship and international awards from 1986-1994 were persuasive evidence relating to this criterion but did not establish the petitioner's sustained acclaim as of March 2005 when the petition was filed. Regarding the alleged Olympic silver medal, the AAO noted counsel's assertions do not constitute evidence (citing Matter of Obaigbena and Matter of Ramirez-Sanchez) and the record showed she actually placed 7th. Regarding NAIA recognition, the AAO held the petitioner bears the burden of demonstrating her awards are nationally recognized, and she submitted no evidence that the NAIA enjoys comparable media coverage to other collegiate tournaments such as coverage in major sports magazines with national circulation. Citing supplementary information at 56 Fed. Reg. 60899, the AAO noted that even major league athletes are not automatically presumed eligible, so playing in a league that is not the top league carries even less weight. The NAIA has 330 members compared to NCAA's 1,024 active members. The AAO found the petitioner did not establish this criterion with evidence of sustained acclaim as of filing in March 2005.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel asserted the petitioner meets this criterion through her membership on the China National Team (through membership in the Chinese Volleyball Association), playing for an NAIA team, and her selection as Most Valuable Player and for the All-American team. Materials submitted on appeal showed NAIA requires only that incoming freshmen have graduated in the top half of their class, achieved a minimum SAT or ACT score, and attained a high school GPA of at least 2.0.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO acknowledged that membership on a national team, as opposed to one of several major league teams, can serve to meet this criterion, but the petitioner must demonstrate sustained acclaim. The director had concluded the petitioner had not established the membership criteria for the Chinese Volleyball Association, and counsel did not directly address this on appeal. The NAIA requirements (top half of class, minimum SAT/ACT, 2.0 GPA) are not outstanding achievements. While the AAO acknowledged selection for All American and All Tournament teams, the petitioner had not addressed the concern that the NAIA does not have the same national recognition as the NCAA. Being selected among 330 NAIA teams carries less weight than a similar selection by the NCAA, which has more than three times as many members. The petitioner submitted little evidence of sustained acclaim relating to this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted several news articles in unidentified Chinese newspapers, another foreign-language article, sports coverage on college websites, and articles that appeared in the Fresno Bee. A 1999 article in an unidentified foreign language newspaper and some Fresno Bee articles appeared to be primarily about the petitioner.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted that the record does not contain any evidence regarding the circulation of the papers or translations of the foreign-language articles, and counsel did not contest this on appeal. The Fresno Bee appears to be a local paper. Most of the articles were reporting results of competitions in which the petitioner played rather than being primarily about her. The exceptions (a 1999 foreign language article and some Fresno Bee articles) did not suffice because the Fresno Bee is purely local and the record lacked evidence regarding the unidentified foreign language publication. The petitioner did not establish this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel asserted the petitioner was an 'elite' member of the China National Team. Jenny Ping Lang, Head Coach of the U.S. National Women's Volleyball Team, submitted a letter (containing near identical language to a letter from the petitioner's coach at Fresno Pacific University) asserting the petitioner's key role for the Chinese team was evidenced by her most valuable player award. The record contained confirmation of the petitioner's selection as a member of All American and All Tournament teams as well as most valuable player recognition at Fresno Pacific University.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted the record contains no recognition from China as a most valuable player and Ms. Lang professed no first-hand knowledge of the petitioner's role for the China National Team. The certificates from Fresno Pacific University do not reflect that the petitioner was prospectively selected to play a leading role such as team captain. The AAO disagreed with the director's conclusion that the petitioner met this criterion, reasoning that volleyball is a team sport and not every member of a winning team plays a leading or critical role above and beyond other team members. There is no evidence the petitioner was team captain or held some equivalent role on the Chinese National Team. Recognition for past achievements such as 'most valued player' relates more to the awards criterion, and 'all-star' team membership relates more to the membership criterion, both already addressed. The petitioner did not establish this criterion.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal, finding the petitioner had only arguably satisfied two criteria as an athlete, and even that evidence was not persuasive of sustained acclaim as of March 2005 when the petition was filed. Review of the record did not establish that the petitioner had distinguished herself as a volleyball player to such an extent that she may be said to have achieved sustained national or international acclaim or to be within the small percentage at the very top of her field. Additionally, the petitioner failed to establish she was coming to the United States to continue work in her area of expertise — she is in the U.S. on a student visa pursuing an international business degree, with no evidence she intends to continue competing, and she has no documented coaching experience (and competitive athletics and coaching are not the same area of expertise). The burden of proof remained with the petitioner and she had not sustained that burden.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Lee v. I.N.S., 237 F. Supp. 2d 914 (N.D. Ill. 2002)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Counsel questioned the 'integrity of the adjudication process' on appeal, specifically challenging the director's statement that counsel had referenced the petitioner as a soccer player in the cover letter. The AAO acknowledged that the cover letter accompanying the instant petition made no reference to soccer, but noted the director was apparently referencing a cover letter from a previous petition (WAC 04 133 51860) in which counsel did refer to the petitioner as a soccer player on page 8. The AAO found the director erred in stating counsel made the same mistake in the current cover letter, but this single error was not the basis of the director's decision and did not warrant withdrawal of the entire decision.
